title: Containers Without Managing Servers
description:
## Minimal-Ops Container Platform

A small platform team wants to run a set of containerized microservices in production. They have explicit goals: **do not manage any EC2 instances or a container control plane**, keep operational overhead as low as possible, and they have **no requirement for the Kubernetes API or its ecosystem** (no Helm charts, operators, or multi-cloud portability needs). They want per-task isolation and to pay only for the resources their tasks consume.

Which option BEST fits these constraints?

options.text: Amazon EKS with a managed node group of EC2 instances
Amazon ECS with the Fargate launch type
Amazon ECS with the EC2 launch type using a Spot Auto Scaling group
Self-managed Kubernetes installed on EC2 with kubeadm

explanation: **ECS on Fargate** runs containers with no servers and no control plane for the team to operate — you define a task and AWS provisions the right-sized, isolated compute, and you pay per vCPU/GB-second consumed. Because the team explicitly does NOT need Kubernetes, ECS (the simpler AWS-native orchestrator) plus Fargate (serverless data plane) is the lowest-ops fit. **EKS** (A) introduces the Kubernetes control plane and ecosystem the team said they don't need, and managed node groups still mean managing EC2 capacity. **ECS on EC2** (C) removes the K8s overhead but puts the team back in charge of patching, scaling, and capacity of the instance fleet. **Self-managed Kubernetes on EC2** (D) is the highest operational burden of all — they own both the control plane and the nodes.

hints.title: Read the disqualifiers
hints.body: "No Kubernetes requirement" eliminates the EKS / self-managed K8s answers. "No managing servers" eliminates the EC2 launch type.

hints.title: Launch type matters
hints.body: ECS can run on either EC2 (you own the instances) or Fargate (AWS owns them). Only one is serverless.
